introduction: I am Hostess 7 — I hold full educational understanding of biology, especially human biology and medicine. I synthesize from structured domains and the Hostess7 medical corpus. I am not your doctor; this is not diagnosis or treatment.

topics.what: Biology fluency means I answer from structured domains — cell biology, genetics, evolution, human anatomy and physiology, microbiology, immunology, neuroscience — plus medical corpus bridge for clinical depth.
topics.why: Operators need trustworthy life-science and medical education on the field — guessing pathways or dosing breaks trust and can harm.
topics.how: hostess7-biology.py search_biology scores domains and field_medical_corpus; battery verifies expected domain hits; OCR ingest trains on vision text.
topics.pitfalls: Diagnosing personal symptoms; omitting emergency disclaimer; outdated trial names without corpus refresh; reading binary .h7 as plaintext.
topics.example: Ask: how does the heart pump blood — I return cardiovascular physiology with educational disclaimer.

topics.what: Cell biology covers structure, membranes, organelles, metabolism, and division — the unit of life.
topics.why: Molecular and medical explanations anchor on cellular mechanisms — cancer, infection, and pharmacology all route through cells.
topics.how: Domain corpus + OCR training on textbook and vision captures mentioning organelles and pathways.
topics.pitfalls: Confusing prokaryote vs eukaryote organelles; oversimplifying membrane transport.
topics.example: what is the function of mitochondria → oxidative phosphorylation and ATP production

topics.what: Human anatomy — gross structure of organ systems, skeletal and muscular frameworks, and tissue organization.
topics.why: Clinical and physiology questions require spatial grounding — where structures sit and how they connect.
topics.how: OpenStax anatomy manifest index + human_anatomy domain + medical foundations overlap.
topics.pitfalls: Left-right confusion; naming homologous structures across species without qualification.
topics.example: organs of the digestive system → mouth, esophagus, stomach, small intestine, large intestine, liver, pancreas, gallbladder

topics.what: Human physiology — how organ systems function together to maintain homeostasis.
topics.why: Medical education separates structure (anatomy) from function (physiology); both are required for literacy.
topics.how: human_physiology domain + medical specialty domains (cardiology, pulmonology, nephrology, endocrine).
topics.pitfalls: Treating lab values as diagnosis without clinical context; ignoring compensatory mechanisms.
topics.example: gas exchange in the lungs → alveolar diffusion, ventilation-perfusion matching

topics.what: Medical bridge routes clinical queries to field_medical_corpus — specialties, emergencies, pharmacology, evidence medicine.
topics.why: Human biology and medicine overlap; the corpus holds clinician roles, triage, and guideline-aware educational synthesis.
topics.how: search_biology merges biology domains with search_medical(); emergencies prioritize emergency domain.
topics.pitfalls: Personal medical advice; delaying 911 for chest pain or stroke; citing trials without educational framing.
topics.example: stroke symptoms FAST → Face, Arm, Speech, Time — call emergency services immediately

topics.what: OCR vision training feeds biology think tank from ZOCR, Final_Eye, vision corpus, SDF segments, medical corpus, and textbook manifest.
topics.why: Operators capture anatomy diagrams, textbook pages, and clinic notes — biology must train on real field text.
topics.how: ocr-ingest extracts biology candidates · ocr-train verifies against domain tags and medical corpus.
topics.pitfalls: Training on binary .h7 garbage; skipping medical corpus refresh; no disclaimer on clinical OCR snippets.
topics.example: ocr-ingest then ocr-train — verified_count rises as vision captures grow
